questions.q: By the early 1960s, accelerators had revealed over a hundred 'fundamental' particles. Why did physicists find this 'particle zoo' unsatisfactory?
options: The particles were all unstable and decayed too fast to study
Truly fundamental building blocks should be few in number; a hundred of them suggests a deeper underlying structure rather than a real answer
The particles violated conservation of charge
They could not be detected reliably
questions.explanation: Fundamental particles are meant to be a small, clean set from which everything is built. A hundred 'fundamental' things is the absence of an answer, not an answer — it hinted that these particles were really combinations of something more basic, which turned out to be quarks.

questions.q: Gell-Mann's 'Eightfold Way' did more than tidy up known particles — it made a prediction. What was the omega-minus?
options: A new force-carrying particle predicted by the pattern
A missing particle at an empty corner of a geometric pattern, whose charge, strangeness and mass Gell-Mann predicted before it was found
The first antimatter particle ever observed
A new type of accelerator built at Brookhaven
questions.explanation: One slot at the tip of the baryon decuplet was empty. The pattern was so tight that Gell-Mann predicted the missing particle's charge (-1), strangeness (-3) and mass. Its discovery at Brookhaven in 1964, with exactly those properties, confirmed the Eightfold Way — a prediction, not mere bookkeeping.

questions.q: A proton is made of two up quarks and one down quark. Given quark charges of \(+\tfrac{2}{3}\) (up) and \(-\tfrac{1}{3}\) (down), what is the proton's total charge?
questions.explanation: \(+\tfrac{2}{3} + \tfrac{2}{3} - \tfrac{1}{3} = +\tfrac{3}{3} = +1\), the observed proton charge. The neutron (up, down, down) gives \(+\tfrac{2}{3} - \tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{1}{3} = 0\). The fractional quark charges combine to the whole-number charges we actually observe.

questions.q: Why were quarks initially dismissed as a mere mathematical convenience rather than real particles?
options: Their predicted masses were impossibly large
No free quark — and no fractional charge — had ever been observed, despite searches in cosmic rays, seawater and rocks
They violated the conservation of energy
Gell-Mann himself never published the idea
questions.explanation: Every particle ever seen carried a whole-number multiple of \(e\). Quarks needed fractional charges (\(\pm\tfrac{1}{3}, \pm\tfrac{2}{3}\)), and no free quark turned up in any search. To most physicists they were an accounting trick of the symmetry algebra — until deep inelastic scattering showed otherwise.

questions.q: To probe inside a proton (~1 fm across), SLAC needed very high-energy electrons. Which principle explains why?
options: Higher-energy electrons are hotter and melt the proton
A particle's de Broglie wavelength \( \lambda = h/p \) shrinks as momentum rises, and you need \( \lambda \) smaller than the structure you wish to resolve
High energy makes electrons positively charged
Only high-energy electrons can pass through liquid hydrogen
questions.explanation: By de Broglie, \(\lambda = h/p\); higher momentum means shorter wavelength. To resolve sub-proton structure you need a wavelength smaller than ~1 fm, which demands electrons of several GeV and beyond — exactly why SLAC accelerated electrons up to 20 GeV.

questions.q: The SLAC deep inelastic scattering experiment was conceptually a repeat of which earlier landmark, one level deeper?
options: Millikan's oil-drop experiment
Rutherford's gold-foil experiment, which revealed the nucleus by scattering
Young's double-slit experiment
The photoelectric effect experiment
questions.explanation: Just as Rutherford fired alpha particles at gold foil and saw large-angle scattering revealing a small hard nucleus, SLAC fired electrons at protons and saw unexpectedly frequent large-angle (deep inelastic) scattering — revealing small, hard, point-like objects (quarks) inside the proton.

questions.q: If the proton were a soft, diffuse blob of charge, electrons would rarely scatter at large angles. What did Friedman, Kendall and Taylor actually observe in deep inelastic scattering?
options: Almost no large-angle scattering, confirming a soft proton
Far more frequent hard, large-angle scattering than expected, indicating small hard point-like constituents inside the proton
The electrons passed straight through with no deflection at all
The protons were completely destroyed before any scattering occurred
questions.explanation: The hard scatters did not fall away as a soft blob would require — electrons bounced off at large angles far more often than predicted. The only explanation, echoing Rutherford, was small hard point-like kernels inside the proton: the quarks. The proton was a 'bag of marbles', not a smooth ball.

questions.q: Why can a single free quark never be isolated (colour confinement)?
options: Quarks decay instantly into electrons
As you pull two quarks apart the strong force does not weaken; the stored energy grows until it converts into a new quark-antiquark pair, so you only ever make more particles
Quarks are too small for any detector to register
The electromagnetic force binds them too tightly
questions.explanation: Unlike gravity or electromagnetism, the strong force between quarks does not fade with distance — the flux-tube energy keeps growing as you pull them apart. Eventually that energy (via \(E=mc^2\)) creates a new quark-antiquark pair at the break point, giving new colour-neutral particles instead of a free quark. You can never win.

questions.q: What is the property of 'asymptotic freedom', which explained why partons looked like free particles in the SLAC collisions?
options: Quarks become heavier when close together
The strong force between quarks becomes very weak when they are very close together (at high energy), so they behave almost freely
Quarks lose their colour charge at high energy
The strong force becomes infinite at short range
questions.explanation: Asymptotic freedom means the strong force nearly vanishes at very short distances and high energies, so closely packed quarks rattle around almost freely — exactly why Feynman's partons appeared free in the violent SLAC scatters. Conversely the force is strong at long range, which causes confinement.

questions.q: In terms of quark content, what is a baryon, and what holds it together?
options: A quark-antiquark pair held by the electromagnetic force
Three quarks (e.g. the proton and neutron) bound together in a colour-neutral combination by the strong force
A single quark surrounded by electrons
Two quarks held by gravity
questions.explanation: A baryon is three quarks bound by the strong force in a colour-neutral (red+green+blue) bundle — the proton (uud) and neutron (udd) are baryons. A quark-antiquark pair, by contrast, is a meson. A lone quark is never colour-neutral, so it cannot exist alone.
